title: Default, Built-In, or Generic Account Usage on BES Hosts

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.2.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.35: Default, Built-In, or Generic Account Usage on BES Hosts.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.35' running on index=wineventlog EventCode=4624, generic_accounts_blocklist.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=4624 earliest=-7d
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv ComputerName AS host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| lookup generic_accounts_blocklist.csv Account_Name OUTPUT blocked_account
| where isnotnull(blocked_account)
| stats count by Account_Name, ComputerName, bes_system
| sort - count
description: We flag logons using default or generic accounts that should be disabled or renamed per system hardening standards.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Populate blocklist with Administrator, Guest, vendor defaults. (2) Tune for renamed admin accounts via SID lookup if available. (3) Page IR on hits. (4) Document approved break-glass in lookup. Indexes required: `index=wineventlog`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`.
visualization: Table (violations), Single value (event count), Bar chart (by account). Key fields: `Account_Name`, `ComputerName`, `bes_system`.
knownFalsePositives: Baseline changes during scheduled patches, controlled config updates, or planned firmware upgrades.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=wineventlog` `EventCode=4624`, `generic_accounts_blocklist.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Populate blocklist with Administrator, Guest, vendor defaults. (2) Tune for renamed admin accounts via SID lookup if available. (3) Page IR on hits. (4) Document approved break-glass in lookup.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=4624 earliest=-7d
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv ComputerName AS host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| lookup generic_accounts_blocklist.csv Account_Name OUTPUT blocked_account
| where isnotnull(blocked_account)
| stats count by Account_Name, ComputerName, bes_system
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Default, Built-In, or Generic Account Usage on BES Hosts (CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.2)** — We flag logons using default or generic accounts that should be disabled or renamed per system hardening standards.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=wineventlog` `EventCode=4624`, `generic_accounts_blocklist.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: wineventlog; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Security. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=wineventlog, sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(bes_system)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(blocked_account)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by Account_Name, ComputerName, bes_system** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ESP firewall log, Windows Security, or the asset register—whichever the search is built to mirror. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (violations), Single value (event count), Bar chart (by account)
grandmaExplanation: We flag logons using default or generic accounts that should be disabled or renamed per system hardening standards. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
